content:
# Joshua Chapter 5 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 228-229 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_005_page_001.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 165-166 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0169

---

## The Courage of the Kings Dissolves

**1** When all the Amorite kings west of the Jordan and all the Canaanite kings along the coast heard that YHWH had dried up the Jordan River before the sons of Israel until they had crossed over, their hearts melted. They had no fight left in them because of the sons of Israel.

---

## Circumcision at Gilgal

**2** At that time YHWH said to Joshua, "Make flint knives and circumcise the sons of Israel again."

**3** So Joshua made flint knives and circumcised the sons of Israel at the Hill of Foreskins.

---

**4** Here is why Joshua had to do this: Every male who had come out of Egypt — every man of fighting age — had died in the wilderness during the journey from Egypt. **5** All the men who left Egypt had been circumcised, but none of the boys born in the wilderness along the way had ever been circumcised. **6** For forty years the sons of Israel wandered in the wilderness until the entire generation of fighting men who had left Egypt had perished — because they refused to listen to the voice of YHWH. YHWH had sworn they would never see the land he had promised their ancestors, a land flowing with milk and honey. **7** So it was their sons — the ones YHWH raised up to take their place — whom Joshua circumcised, because they had never undergone the rite during the journey.

---

**8** When the entire nation had been circumcised, they stayed where they were in the camp until they had healed.

**9** Then YHWH said to Joshua, "Today I have rolled the disgrace of Egypt off of you." And so that place was called Gilgal — and it is called that to this day.

---

## Passover on the Plains of Jericho

**10** The sons of Israel camped at Gilgal, and on the evening of the fourteenth day of the month, they celebrated the Passover on the plains of Jericho. **11** The day after the Passover they ate from the produce of the land — unleavened bread and roasted grain — on that very day. **12** The manna stopped the next morning, once they had eaten from the land's produce. There was no more manna for the sons of Israel. From then on, they ate from the harvest of the land of Canaan that year.

---

## The Commander of YHWH's Army

**13** When Joshua was near Jericho, he looked up and saw a man standing in front of him with a drawn sword in his hand. Joshua went up to him and said, "Are you on our side, or on theirs?"

**14** "Neither," the man said. "I have come as commander of the army of YHWH."

Joshua fell facedown to the ground in reverence and said, "What does my lord wish to say to his servant?"

**15** The commander of YHWH's army said to Joshua, "Take off your sandal. The place where you are standing is holy."

And Joshua did so.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Joshua 5 — the chapter where the wilderness ends

This is one of the most transitional chapters in the Hebrew Bible. Nothing in it is incidental. The circumcision, the Passover, the cessation of the manna, the theophany — each event is a threshold. The wilderness generation is dead. The wilderness food has stopped. The wilderness state of the body — uncircumcised, outside the covenant's physical sign — is corrected. And then a sword-bearing figure appears who refuses to take sides. The old world is finished. The new world has not yet been conquered. Joshua 5 is the space between.

---

### Verse 1 — "their hearts melted"

**וַיִּמַּס לְבָבָם** — "and their heart melted."

The verb **מָסַס** (*masas*) means to melt, to dissolve, to become liquid. It is the same verb Rahab used in chapter 2 when she told the Israelite spies, "Our hearts melted and no one had any courage left because of you." What Rahab reported as one woman's testimony now extends to every king in the region. The drying of the Jordan has completed the parallel with the drying of the Sea of Reeds — both water-crossings on dry ground, both producing the same dissolution of enemy will. The phrase "there was no longer spirit in them" uses **רוּחַ** (*ruach*) in the sense of fighting resolve, the inner breath of courage. It has gone out of them.

The Qere/Ketiv here is striking. The Ketiv reads **עָבְרֵנוּ** — "until *we* crossed over" — first person plural, as though the narrator is among those who crossed. The Qere corrects to **עָבְרָם** — "until *they* crossed." I follow the Qere, but the Ketiv preserves a remarkable thing: the voice of someone who was there.

The LXX Vaticanus renders the melting as **κατεπλάγησαν** — "they were struck with terror" — which is weaker. The Hebrew image is visceral: hearts becoming liquid. Terror so total it dissolves the solid self. The Greek flattens this to mere shock.

---

### Verse 2 — flint knives

**חַרְבוֹת צֻרִים** — "knives of flint."

Flint, not bronze or iron. This is not merely archaic technology — flint was specifically used for circumcision in the ancient Near East. The specification connects directly to Exodus 4:25, where Zipporah seizes a flint and circumcises her son. The ritual conservatism is deliberate: sacred acts preserve ancient instruments long after newer tools exist for everyday use.

**שֵׁנִית** — "a second time" — does not mean each man was circumcised twice. It means the nation as a whole underwent circumcision for the second time: the first generation had been circumcised in Egypt; this second generation, born in the wilderness, had never received the rite.

The LXX renders **μαχαίρας πετρίνας** — "stone knives" — which is accurate but generic. The Hebrew **צֻרִים** specifically denotes flint, not just any stone.

---

### Verse 3 — the Hill of Foreskins

**גִּבְעַת הָעֲרָלוֹת** — "the Hill of the Foreskins."

Hebrew place-naming is startlingly physical. The hill near Gilgal received its name from what happened there: a mass circumcision, foreskins piled at the site. The Bible does not euphemize its sacred geography. What happened at a place names the place.

---

### Verses 4-7 — the logic of two generations

The parenthetical explanation in these verses is syntactically dense, sorting carefully between those who "went out" from Egypt and those who were "born" in the wilderness. The first generation — circumcised, disobedient, dead. The second generation — uncircumcised, untested, alive. The circumcision at Gilgal reconstitutes Israel as a covenant people: the generation that enters the land now bears the covenant sign in their flesh, as Abraham's household did when first commanded in Genesis 17.

The text does not explain why the wilderness generation was never circumcised. It simply states the fact: **לֹא-מָלוּ** — "they did not circumcise." Rabbinic tradition offers explanations — the north wind that aids healing did not blow during the wilderness years (Talmud Yevamot 71b-72a), or the covenant sign was suspended during the period of divine displeasure. The text itself is silent on the reason.

---

### Verse 9 — "I have rolled the disgrace of Egypt off of you"

**גַּלּוֹתִי אֶת-חֶרְפַּת מִצְרַיִם מֵעֲלֵיכֶם** — "I have rolled away the reproach of Egypt from upon you."

The verb **גַּלּוֹתִי** (*galloti*) comes from the root **גָּלַל** (*galal*) — to roll. The place-name **גִּלְגָּל** (Gilgal) derives from the same root. The wordplay is explicit and forms an etiology: the place is called Gilgal because YHWH *rolled away* the disgrace there.

What is "the disgrace of Egypt"? The phrase is deliberately ambiguous:

1. **The shame Egypt placed on Israel** — the reproach of servitude, the identity of a slave-people who could never hold their own land or practice their own covenant.
2. **The shame of resembling the Egyptians** — the uncircumcised state. Without the covenant sign, the wilderness generation looked, in this one respect, like the nations around them.
3. **The taunt Egypt could speak** — the mockery that YHWH brought his people out only to let them die in the desert (cf. Exodus 32:12; Numbers 14:13-16).

I have translated it as "the disgrace of Egypt" rather than "the reproach of Egypt" because *disgrace* carries more emotional weight in contemporary English — it is something that sticks to you, something that must be actively removed. The Hebrew **חֶרְפָּה** (*cherpah*) is precisely that: a shame that clings until it is stripped away. YHWH rolls it off like a stone from a grave.

The LXX cannot reproduce the *galal/Gilgal* wordplay. It transliterates the place-name as Γαλγαλα and the connection is lost in Greek.

---

### Verses 10-12 — the Passover, and the day the manna stopped

Three things happen in quick succession, and each one is a threshold:

First, the Passover — **הַפֶּסַח** — celebrated on the fourteenth of the month, on the plains of Jericho. This is the first Passover in the promised land. The last recorded Passover was at Sinai (Numbers 9:1-5). Between those two celebrations lies forty years of wilderness.

Second, the eating of the land's produce — **מֵעֲבוּר הָאָרֶץ** — unleavened bread and roasted grain, on the day after the Passover. The word **עֲבוּר** (*avur*) means produce or grain — presumably the stored harvest left by the land's inhabitants. The people eat what the land yields rather than what heaven drops.

Third, the manna ceases — **וַיִּשְׁבֹּת הַמָּן**. The verb is **שָׁבַת** (*shavat*) — the same root as Sabbath. The manna *rests*. It ceases its work. The manna began in Exodus 16, roughly forty years earlier, and now it stops — precisely on the day after they first eat the land's produce. There is no gap. Heaven's provision ends exactly when the land's provision begins. The transition is seamless. YHWH who fed them with bread from the sky now feeds them through the earth's harvest.

I have rendered the manna's cessation plainly — "the manna stopped" — because the English word *ceased* carries a formality that the Hebrew does not require. The manna simply stopped. It had done its work.

---

### Verses 13-15 — the man with the drawn sword

This is one of the strangest and most powerful encounters in the Hebrew Bible.

**וְהִנֵּה-אִישׁ עֹמֵד לְנֶגְדּוֹ וְחַרְבּוֹ שְׁלוּפָה בְּיָדוֹ** — "and look — a man standing opposite him and his sword drawn in his hand."

The figure is identified only as **אִישׁ** (*ish*) — a man. Not an angel, not a divine messenger. A man with a drawn sword. Joshua asks the field commander's question: *Whose side are you on?*

The answer is the most theologically charged single word in the chapter: **לֹא** — "No."

I have rendered this as "Neither" rather than the literal "No" because the English "No" in response to "Are you on our side or theirs?" sounds like a refusal to answer. The Hebrew **לֹא** here is a refusal of the binary itself. The figure is not declining to respond — he is rejecting the premise. He is not *for* Israel in the way Joshua means. He is not a recruit. He is **שַׂר-צְבָא-יְהוָה** — "commander of the army of YHWH." The army belongs to YHWH, not to Israel. The conquest is YHWH's campaign, and Israel participates in it — not the reverse.

This reframing is the theological heart of the passage. Joshua thinks he is asking a military question. He gets a theological answer. The one standing before him does not serve Israel's strategy. Israel serves his.

**The parallel with Exodus 3 is unmistakable.** The command to remove the sandal because the ground is holy echoes Moses at the burning bush nearly word for word: "Remove your sandal from your foot, for the place where you are standing is holy" (5:15) parallels "Remove your sandals from your feet, for the place where you are standing is holy ground" (Exodus 3:5). Joshua is being placed in Moses's role. As Moses received his commission before the liberation from Egypt, Joshua receives his before the conquest of Canaan. The succession is complete.

Note the singular in the MT — **נַעַלְךָ** ("your sandal") and **רַגְלֶךָ** ("your foot") — versus the plural in Exodus 3:5. The LXX harmonizes to the plural, matching the Exodus passage. The Masoretic text preserves the distinction. I have kept the singular ("your sandal") as it stands in the Leningrad Codex.

The identity of the figure is debated and unresolved:

- An angel of high rank, commanding the heavenly hosts (cf. Daniel 10:13, 12:1, where Michael is called "the great prince").
- The Angel of YHWH — the figure from the burning bush, to whom divine attributes are given. The Exodus 3 parallel supports this identification strongly.
- A divine manifestation — YHWH himself in warrior form. Joshua's prostration is not rebuked, unlike the angel's correction of John in Revelation 19:10 and 22:8-9. The absence of rebuke has been read as evidence that this figure accepts worship because he is divine.

The Targum Jonathan explicitly identifies the figure as an angel. The Hebrew text does not.

**The chapter ends abruptly.** Joshua does as he is told — removes his sandal — and the chapter stops. No instructions are given. No battle plan is revealed. The Septuagint continues directly into the Jericho instructions (Joshua 6:2-5), suggesting the chapter break is artificial. The Masoretic text leaves the reader suspended in the encounter. The silence is deliberate. Joshua stands barefoot on holy ground, and the next word has not yet been spoken.

---

### A note on "the LORD" vs. YHWH

As throughout the PNEUMA Bible, I render the divine name as YHWH rather than "the LORD." The four-letter name appears throughout this chapter — in the melting of the kings' hearts (v. 1), in the command to circumcise (v. 2), in the rolling away of disgrace (v. 9), and in the title of the sword-bearing commander (v. 14). The name is not a title. It is the name.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 228-229, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Joshua 5 begins in the left column of page 228 and continues through the columns of page 229. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 165-166, accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0169. The Vaticanus LXX is in general agreement with the Leningrad Hebrew throughout Joshua 5, with notable divergences in vocabulary (κατεπλάγησαν for וַיִּמַּס in v. 1; ἀρχιστράτηγος for שַׂר in v. 14) and harmonization to the plural in v. 15.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
